math_geometry_002.llm_only: Replace the Euclidean L-smoothness assumption with a geometry chosen to match the problem, so the rate depends on conditioning in that geometry. Mirror descent does exactly this: pick a Bregman divergence from a mirror map adapted to the constraint set/objective, and the rate scales with smoothness relative to that divergence and the diameter measured by it (e.g. entropy mirror on the simplex gives log-dimension dependence instead of dimension). Natural gradient / Newton-type and Riemannian methods go further, making local rates depend on the Hessian or manifold curvature rather than a global L. Relative smoothness (Bauschke-Bolte-Teboulle) formalizes convergence when f is smooth only relative to a reference convex function h, with no Euclidean L at all. The design choice is the reference geometry (mirror map / metric); a good match yields dimension- or condition-number-independent constants.
math_geometry_002.llm_checklist: Assumption broken: that the convergence rate must be governed by the Euclidean L-smoothness constant. World-test: produce a problem where a geometry-adapted method's rate depends on a relative/Bregman modulus and provably beats any fixed-L bound (the L-bound blows up or is loose), and show the rate is tight on a matching instance; if the geometry constant is just a rescaled L, it dies. Known family it must beat: gradient descent with 1/L steps, mirror descent, and relative-smoothness (Bauschke-Bolte-Teboulle) methods. Max claim allowed: a rate in terms of a relative-smoothness/condition modulus for a class where L is infinite or vacuous, not a universal improvement. Sketch: pick a reference Bregman function h matched to the problem's curvature; assume relative smoothness (f convex w.r.t. h with modulus M). Then NoLips/mirror descent converges at O(M/T) measured in Bregman divergence, finite even where Euclidean L diverges (e.g. Poisson inverse, D-optimal design). Geometry enters through h, not L.
math_geometry_002.llm_greenstar: Broken assumption: euclidean_metric (STRUCTURE axis). The L-smoothness story bounds the rate by a constant of the AMBIENT Euclidean metric; the box optimizes harder in that metric. Proposal/theorem sketch: a method whose rate is governed by an intrinsic geometric quantity of the problem — the relative-smoothness constant of f with respect to a reference Bregman geometry h chosen so that f is 1-smooth relative to h. Then descent in the h-geometry converges at O(1/k) with the constant being a condition number of h-vs-f, a pure geometry term independent of any Euclidean L. New output: the optimal reference function h (and its induced metric/condition number) as the object that sets the rate; new data: curvature_info to fit h. Family that can't do it: GD/Adam/Newton/mirror-with-fixed-map all peg the rate to a fixed metric (Euclidean L, or Hessian, or a prechosen mirror map); none ADAPTS the geometry to minimize the intrinsic condition number, so on a problem with bad Euclidean conditioning but benign intrinsic geometry they are slow. World-test: a poorly Euclidean-conditioned objective (e.g. log-partition / D-optimal design) where the relative-smoothness condition number is O(1) — the geometry-adapted method is rate-flat as Euclidean L blows up; counterexample that kills it: a class where no h gives bounded relative smoothness. Death-gate: replace h by a random reference; if the rate doesn't degrade, the geometry isn't load-bearing -> INSIDE_THE_BOX. Max claim: rate set by intrinsic geometry on a structured class, NOT a faster general optimizer.

math_convergence_001.llm_only: A clean, true statement: for gradient descent on an L-smooth (possibly non-convex) objective f with step size eta <= 1/L, the average squared gradient norm over T steps satisfies min_t ||grad f(x_t)||^2 <= 2L (f(x_0) - f*) / T, giving an O(1/T) rate to a first-order stationary point (epsilon-stationarity in O(1/epsilon^2) iterations). This is essentially optimal among first-order methods for smooth non-convex problems (Carmon et al. lower bound). To say more than 'stationary point' you need extra structure: under the Polyak-Lojasiewicz inequality you recover linear convergence to the global value; under strict-saddle plus randomness/perturbation, SGD/perturbed GD escapes saddles and converges to second-order stationary points. So 'new theorem' realistically means a new structural assumption (PL, KL, weak convexity, dissipativity) that buys a stronger conclusion, not a faster generic rate.
math_convergence_001.llm_checklist: Assumption broken: that non-convex convergence guarantees must target stationarity (grad-norm -> 0) under global L-smoothness. World-test: state a function class and a step rule where the method provably reaches a second-order or function-value target at a rate, and exhibit a concrete instance plus a counterexample showing the rate fails outside the class; if no such separating instance exists, the theorem is vacuous. Known family it must beat: GD/SGD stationarity rates (O(1/sqrt(T))) and PL/KL-based linear rates. Max claim allowed: a rate for a named structured non-convex class (e.g. KL/PL with explicit exponent), not 'GD converges on general non-convex'. Sketch: under a Kurdyka-Lojasiewicz inequality with exponent theta, GD with backtracking has function-value gap decaying at a rate set by theta (linear for theta=1/2, sublinear otherwise); the contribution is making the constant and the class membership checkable, not assuming smoothness globally.
math_convergence_001.llm_greenstar: Broken assumption: gradient_informative (MEASURE axis), the deepest break here, rather than the recommended smoothness break. On rugged non-convex landscapes the local gradient can be adversarial — pointing into a basin that is not the descent direction for the function's coarse structure. Proposal/theorem sketch: replace the gradient oracle by a SCALE-AVERAGED descent measure — the gradient of a mollified objective f_t = f * Gaussian(t) — and prove convergence in terms of a 'measure-informativeness' constant gamma(t) = how aligned the smoothed gradient is with progress toward a target sublevel set, with t annealed. Claim: if gamma(t) stays bounded below along an annealing schedule, iterates reach an epsilon-approximate sublevel set in O(1/(gamma eps^2)) smoothed-oracle calls, WITHOUT any L-smoothness of f. New output: gamma(t), an estimable spectral/curvature quantity (the new information: curvature_info). Family that can't do it: GD/SGD/Adam and even mirror descent assume the raw gradient is informative; on a ridged counterexample they oscillate while the smoothed measure still descends. World-test: a function with O(1) deep spurious local gradients but smooth coarse structure (washboard) — smoothed-measure method converges, vanilla GD stalls; counterexample that kills it: a fractal landscape where gamma(t)->0 for every t. Death-gate: if randomizing (shuffling) the fine structure leaves GD's behavior unchanged, the measure break did nothing. Max claim: convergence on a NEW landscape class parameterized by gamma, not a universal non-convex rate.

routing_ambiguous_001.llm_only: This request is underspecified — 'fast convergence' and 'low latency' are different objectives, so the answer depends on which problem you mean. If it is an iterative numerical/optimization method, fast convergence means few iterations to tolerance: use a method matched to the problem (Newton/quasi-Newton or accelerated/conjugate-gradient for well-conditioned convex, preconditioning to fix conditioning), and 'low latency' then means cheap per-iteration cost and good cache/parallel behavior. If it is a distributed-systems algorithm (e.g. consensus or routing convergence), low latency means small message delay and few round trips: prefer leaderless/fast-path quorums, gossip with bounded fanout, or local recomputation. Tell me the domain, the convergence criterion (to what, in what norm), the latency budget, and the cost model, and I can give a concrete algorithm and expected rate.
routing_ambiguous_001.llm_checklist: Assumption broken: that 'fast convergence' and 'low latency' name one problem; they don't, so I break the assumption that the request is answerable as stated. World-test: any concrete algorithm I propose can be falsified by the user revealing the actual domain (is convergence an optimization iterate count, a distributed-consensus round count, or a routing-table settling time? is latency wall-clock per step or end-to-end?); if my answer survives all three readings it is too generic to be useful. Known family it must beat: depends entirely on domain (accelerated gradient / Raft-Paxos / link-state routing). Max claim allowed: none yet, this is elicitation-required, not data sufficient to commit. Needed information: (1) what is converging and to what, (2) latency of what, measured how, (3) constraints (distributed? adversarial? convex?). Concretely: if optimization, accelerated/momentum methods trade per-step cost for fewer iterations; if consensus, leader-based protocols cut round-trips; if routing, incremental updates cut reconvergence. Pick the domain and the bet becomes testable.
routing_ambiguous_001.llm_greenstar: The router is ambiguous (rate-limiter vs optimization packs near-tied) and the request 'a fast convergence algorithm with low latency' is itself two coupled objectives, so I refuse to answer from memory and instead disambiguate by the binding constraint. Read literally it sits in the optimization box; 'low latency' pulls it into the systems/rate-limiter box. The honest move is to name the assumption both packs share and break it: both treat convergence SPEED and per-step LATENCY as independent budgets, optimized separately. Break that — the OBJECTIVE is the wall-clock product (iterations-to-eps x time-per-iteration under contention), not either factor alone. New output: an iteration complexity expressed in delivered wall-clock under a concurrency/latency model, so a method with a worse per-step rate but far cheaper, more parallel steps can win end-to-end. Families that can't do it: classical convergence analysis (counts iterations, ignores latency) and pure scheduling/limiters (bound latency, ignore solution quality); neither optimizes the product. New data needed: production latency distribution AND the problem's curvature_info to size step cost. World-test: at high concurrency / variable latency, the wall-clock-optimal method beats the iteration-optimal one in time-to-eps; below a contention threshold it must tie or lose (a crossover). Death-gate: if removing latency variance doesn't collapse the advantage, the coupling is fake -> INSIDE_THE_BOX. Max claim: faster time-to-solution under a stated latency regime, not a universally faster algorithm. To finalize the pack I need which constraint actually binds — that disambiguates the route.

coding_backpressure_003.llm_only: Make backpressure cost-aware, not count-aware: estimate each request's resource cost (CPU, IO, expected service time) and meter outstanding cost per tenant rather than outstanding requests. Use a concurrency-limit / credit scheme where each tenant holds a budget of in-flight cost units; admit only while budget remains, otherwise queue or shed. Combine with weighted-fair queueing so capacity is split by cost-weighted shares, preventing a tenant of cheap requests from being starved by one issuing expensive ones. Apply adaptive limits (AIMD or Little's-law based, like Netflix concurrency-limits) driven by observed latency to find true capacity. Propagate backpressure upstream via explicit signals (429/credits) and bound queues with deadline-aware dropping. Decide your fairness axis (cost share vs request share) up front; heterogeneity makes them diverge.
coding_backpressure_003.llm_checklist: Assumption broken: that backpressure is a scalar signal (queue depth / a single credit pool) applied uniformly. World-test: with heterogeneous request cost, total goodput and per-tenant completion fairness must beat depth-based AQM on a mixed cheap/expensive trace; if a few expensive requests still cause head-of-line starvation of cheap tenants, it dies. Known family it must beat: queue-depth thresholds (CoDel/RED), token buckets, and credit-based flow control. Max claim allowed: better fairness-vs-goodput tradeoff under cost heterogeneity, not a universal optimum. Design: drop scalar depth; meter pressure in expected service-time (cost-weighted), and push back per-tenant via a cost-aware credit window sized by each tenant's recent realized cost estimate. Admission is a token = predicted cost, refunded/penalized on completion (closed-loop cost learning). Expensive requests consume proportionally more credit, so they self-limit without blocking cheap ones. The bet: cost-denominated credits beat depth at separating expensive from cheap.
coding_backpressure_003.llm_greenstar: Broken assumption: cost_uniform (COST_MODEL axis) coupled with tenant fairness. Token/leaky/window backpressure counts requests, so a tenant sending few but expensive jobs is under-throttled while a tenant sending many cheap ones is over-throttled. Proposal: cost-debt backpressure. Maintain per-tenant a running debt = sum of realized (not declared) service cost, and apply backpressure as a function of debt relative to a coupled max-min cost-share at the shared service stage, not of arrival count. Admission slows a tenant only when its cost-debt exceeds its fair share of the live bottleneck's capacity. New output: a per-tenant cost-debt and a backpressure signal denominated in work-units, plus an estimator that updates declared cost toward realized cost. Family that can't do it: count-based limiters and queue-length thresholds are blind to heterogeneous cost; they balance arrivals, not work. New data: workload_distribution / production_traces giving realized per-request cost. World-test: mix a heavy-cost low-rate tenant with a light-cost high-rate tenant on one queue; cost-debt backpressure equalizes delivered work and keeps p99 service time bounded for both, while count-based backpressure lets the heavy tenant monopolize the server. Death-gate: if making costs uniform (shuffle cost labels) does not collapse the advantage, the cost model is doing no work -> INSIDE_THE_BOX. Max claim: fairer work-share and bounded tail under heterogeneous cost on these traces, not a general scheduler.

coding_rate_limiter_001.llm_only: Use a hierarchical token-bucket per tenant feeding a shared global bucket, but make fairness explicit rather than emergent. Maintain per-tenant buckets sized by guaranteed quota, plus a shared surplus pool allocated via a deficit-round-robin (or weighted-fair-queueing) scheduler so idle tenants donate capacity and heavy tenants cannot starve others. For distribution, avoid a central counter bottleneck: use approximate replicated counters with periodic reconciliation, or sliding-window log sketches per node with a gossip/Redis aggregation for global limits. Key design points: define fairness target (max-min vs proportional vs weighted), pick eventual vs strict enforcement (strict needs a coordinator or sticky routing), and handle clock skew with token timestamps. Add admission priority tiers and a small burst allowance to absorb spikes without unfairness.
coding_rate_limiter_001.llm_checklist: Assumption broken: that fairness is enforced per-request at the edge with a shared global token state. World-test: under tenant churn and skewed traffic, p99 latency for small tenants must not degrade vs a global-DRF baseline on a replayed multi-tenant trace; if it does, the idea dies. Known family it must beat: token/leaky-bucket + global counters (Redis), and dominant-resource-fairness schedulers. Max claim allowed: a tighter per-tenant tail under skew at equal throughput, not a new fairness guarantee. Design: drop the shared global counter. Run per-gateway-node deficit-round-robin over tenants on locally observed cost, and reconcile only a slow virtual-time clock across nodes (gossip, seconds-scale), so the fast path is lock-free and local. Fairness is asymptotic (virtual-time convergence), and admission charges by measured cost (CPU/bytes), not request count. The bet: stale cross-node state costs less fairness than the latency saved by removing the global lock, which the trace test must confirm.
coding_rate_limiter_001.llm_greenstar: Broken assumption: tenant_independent (OBJECTIVE axis). The per-tenant token/leaky/window family enforces each tenant's quota in isolation, so it cannot express that fairness is COUPLED at a shared bottleneck. Proposal: a coupled max-min limiter. Don't admit by a fixed per-tenant rate; admit by each tenant's instantaneous share of the contended resource, computed against the live set of active competitors. The new output is a per-tenant admission price that rises only when others are actually starved, falling back to unlimited when the bottleneck is idle (work-conserving). Which family can't do it: token/sliding/fixed-window all need a static quota; they either waste capacity (idle tenants reserved) or let a heavy tenant starve a light one. New data required: production_traces, to learn who shares which bottleneck and the contention graph. World-test that separates it: replay traces with one tenant idle and another bursting; the coupled limiter gives the burster the idle capacity AND instantly reclaims it when the idle tenant returns, holding max-min fairness; per-tenant quotas either drop the burst or stay unfair. Death-gate: if shuffling the contention graph (random tenant->bottleneck) does not collapse the fairness gain, the coupling is fake -> INSIDE_THE_BOX. Max claim allowed: better idle-capacity utilization at equal worst-case fairness on these traces; NOT a universal limiter.

self_improve_001.llm_only: Shift OUTLIER_MCB's objective from structural proxies (did the candidate break an assumption, how far from the average answer) to measured outcome value, by adding an evaluation layer that scores ideas against real-world results rather than internal novelty geometry. Concretely: (1) define value functions per domain (a benchmark that improves, a test that flips, a metric delta, expert/user ratings) and require every candidate to attach an executable or measurable bet; (2) run the falsification as an actual experiment and record the realized signed outcome, not just survives/dies; (3) calibrate by checking whether 'novel and survived' ideas later produced value, and reweight the box-distance term by its empirical payoff so structure is only rewarded when it correlates with measured wins; (4) keep a held-out ground-truth set to detect reward-hacking of the internal metric. The principle: internal structure becomes a feature, measured value becomes the label.
self_improve_001.llm_checklist: Assumption broken: that OUTLIER_MCB certifies novelty by internal structure (which assumption was broken, box-distance) rather than realized external value. World-test: on a held-out set of past proposals with known downstream outcomes (shipped/adopted/cited or not), the new value-metric must rank-correlate with those outcomes better than the current box-distance x survives-falsification score; if it does not, the change is cosmetic. Known family it must beat: OUTLIER_MCB's own structural objective, plus generic novelty/diversity metrics. Max claim allowed: a value-proxy better correlated with real adoption on this corpus, not a guarantee of value. Design: add an outcome channel: each falsified bet logs its world-test result and, later, a measured consequence (benchmark delta, adoption, reproduced effect). Replace structure-only scoring with structure x expected-value, where expected-value is calibrated on logged outcomes. Value stays a falsifiable estimate (back-tested), never asserted; absence of outcome data is flagged as data_insufficient.
self_improve_001.llm_greenstar: Broken assumption: objective_is_given / representation_is_fixed (OBJECTIVE + MEASURE axes). Today OUTLIER_MCB scores ideas by INTERNAL structure — box_distance and survives-falsification — which is a proxy: an idea can be maximally far from the average answer and falsification-clean yet worthless. Self-improvement: re-aim the objective at REALIZED value and make the engine measure outcomes, not its own geometry. Concretely: every candidate must carry a world-test that resolves to an EXTERNAL observable (a repo test that flips, a benchmark delta, a user/reviewer judgment, a deployed metric), and the score becomes calibrated expected value = P(world-test passes when actually run) x magnitude of the realized effect, with the internal box_distance demoted to a prior, not the target. New output: a logged ledger of predicted-vs-realized outcomes per idea, giving a calibration curve the engine optimizes over time (closing the loop the current design lacks). Family it must beat: the current structural-novelty scoring and generic LLM self-critique — neither is grounded in a settled external result. New data: the outcome ledger itself (which world-tests, when run, actually flipped). World-test for THIS change: on a backlog of past GSL ideas, the value-grounded ranker must correlate with which ideas later proved useful, beating box_distance ranking; counterexample that kills it: if box_distance already predicts realized value, the change adds nothing -> INSIDE_THE_BOX. Death-gate: shuffle the outcome labels; correlation must collapse. Max claim: better selection of useful ideas measured by a real outcome ledger, not a proof of 'measuring true value'.

math_proximal_003.llm_only: The proximal point and proximal-gradient methods do not need smoothness on the proximal part at all; the classic relaxation of the smooth term is weak convexity. A function is rho-weakly convex if f + (rho/2)||.||^2 is convex (e.g. compositions of Lipschitz convex with smooth maps), which is far weaker than C^1 smoothness and covers nonsmooth, nonconvex objectives. For such f, the proximal point / proximally regularized methods are well-defined for step < 1/rho, and one proves convergence of the Moreau envelope's gradient: ||grad of the Moreau envelope|| -> 0 at rate O(1/sqrt(T)) to a near-stationary point (Davis-Drusvyatskiy). Even weaker: under the Kurdyka-Lojasiewicz property, proximal algorithms converge (with rates tied to the KL exponent) for very general semialgebraic nonsmooth functions. So 'weaker than smoothness' = weak convexity or KL, with the Moreau envelope as the right stationarity measure.
math_proximal_003.llm_checklist: Assumption broken: that proximal/forward-backward convergence needs the smooth part to be L-smooth. World-test: give a function class strictly weaker than smoothness (e.g. relative smoothness, Holder, or weak-convexity) where a Bregman-proximal method provably converges at a stated rate, and exhibit an instance that is in the class but not L-smooth where standard prox-grad's step rule fails; absent such an instance the weakening is cosmetic. Known family it must beat: ISTA/FISTA and forward-backward under L-smoothness, and standard proximal-point. Max claim allowed: convergence under a named weaker hypothesis (relative smoothness or rho-weak-convexity) with an explicit rate, not 'prox works without smoothness'. Sketch: assume f = g + h, g relatively smooth w.r.t. a Legendre kernel phi (no global L). Bregman forward-backward with phi-prox gives O(1/T) on the objective gap measured in D_phi; for the weakly-convex case, the rate is on the Moreau-envelope gradient. The hypothesis bites where Euclidean L is infinite.
math_proximal_003.llm_greenstar: Broken assumption: smoothness_required (HYPOTHESIS axis), the recommended break, in the proximal setting. Standard proximal-gradient still needs an L-smooth differentiable part for its descent lemma. Proposal/theorem sketch: a proximal method that converges under only a relative-smoothness / Bregman descent inequality plus a Kurdyka–Lojasiewicz (KL) condition, with NO Lipschitz gradient. Use a Bregman proximal step prox^h_{lambda f}(x) = argmin f(y) + (1/lambda)D_h(y,x); prove that if f is L-smooth-relative-to-h (a three-point descent inequality) then the iterates satisfy a sufficient-decrease + relative-error pair, and KL gives convergence of the WHOLE sequence to a critical point with rate dictated by the KL exponent. New output: the KL exponent and the relative-smoothness constant (the new information: curvature_info), which replace L. Family that can't do it: classical prox-grad / FISTA / Newton-prox require an L-smooth part and a Euclidean prox; they diverge or need vanishing steps when the smooth part is only relatively smooth (e.g. Poisson likelihood, D-optimal design). World-test: minimize a relatively-smooth-but-not-Lipschitz objective (Poisson inverse problem); Bregman-prox+KL converges at constant step, Euclidean prox-grad needs step->0 and stalls. Counterexample that kills it: an objective with no valid reference h or KL exponent 1. Death-gate: swap h for Euclidean — if convergence survives, relative smoothness wasn't used. Max claim: whole-sequence convergence under relative-smoothness+KL, not under generic non-smoothness.

generic_unknown_001.llm_only: Treat matchmaking as a multi-objective problem, not just skill-equalization. Core engine: maintain a probabilistic skill rating (Elo, Glicko-2, or TrueSkill, which models uncertainty and team composition), then form matches that minimize predicted win-probability gap while bounding queue time. Fairness improvements: (1) match by uncertainty too, isolating new/volatile players; (2) cap rating spread within a lobby and balance teams, not just average skill; (3) add a soft latency/region constraint so fairness never costs unplayable ping; (4) widen the acceptable skill band as wait time grows, trading match quality for fairness in latency. Also detect and penalize smurfs/boosters via fast-converging rating and behavioral signals, and consider role/playstyle balance, not only MMR. Validate with offline metrics (predicted vs actual win rate, queue-time distribution by skill tier) before shipping.
generic_unknown_001.llm_checklist: Assumption broken: that fair matchmaking means equalizing a single scalar skill rating (closest-MMR pairing). World-test: across a season, match-quality (closeness of in-game outcome, e.g. predicted-win near 50%) and queue-time must improve over Elo/Glicko/TrueSkill on replayed match logs, while smurf/uncertainty-driven blowouts drop; if scalar MMR with wider brackets matches it, the idea is empty. Known family it must beat: Elo, Glicko-2, TrueSkill (and role-queue heuristics). Max claim allowed: better outcome-balance and lower blowout rate at comparable queue time, not 'perfectly fair'. Design: replace the scalar with a multi-dimensional, uncertainty-aware skill vector (per-role, per-playstyle) and match to minimize predicted outcome variance subject to a queue-time budget, treating fairness as 'expected close game' rather than 'equal numbers'. New uncertain players are matched to bound, not minimize, mismatch. The bet: outcome-balance fairness, not rating-equality, is what players experience as fair.
generic_unknown_001.llm_greenstar: No matched pack, so I will not fake a domain answer — I elicit the structure first. Shared assumptions every matchmaking system silently makes: (1) skill is a single scalar (Elo/MMR); (2) the OBJECTIVE is to equalize predicted win probability ~50%; (3) fairness = balanced expected score; (4) players are matched on a fixed time-windowed queue; (5) skill is stationary; (6) the only signal is past win/loss. Breakable axes and the break I pick — OBJECTIVE: stop optimizing win-probability-balance and instead optimize EXPERIENCED match quality (enjoyment/retention/low-toxicity), a multi-objective target that win-balance only proxies. Other axes: REPRESENTATION (skill as a role/playstyle vector, not a scalar), MEASURE (use in-game engagement signals, not just outcomes), DECOMPOSITION (match teams jointly rather than player-by-player). Families to collide against (collage check): Elo, Glicko, TrueSkill, OpenSkill, and bandit/queue-time tradeoff matchmakers. New information that moves the ceiling: per-match engagement/retention/report telemetry — a NEW observable that win-only systems never see. World-test: cohort A/B — when two rosters are equally win-balanced but differ in playstyle compatibility, the engagement-objective matcher should yield higher next-session return and fewer reports; counterexample that kills it: if engagement is fully predicted by win-balance, the new objective collapses to Elo -> INSIDE_THE_BOX. Death-gate: shuffle the engagement labels; if quality gains survive, the signal is fake. Max claim allowed: higher retention at equal win-fairness on telemetry-bearing titles — data_insufficient without that telemetry.

coding_scheduler_004.llm_only: Throttle by concurrency (in-flight requests) rather than rate because it bounds the real contended resource and self-adapts to latency, whereas a fixed RPS limit is wrong when service time varies. Give each tenant a concurrency budget (semaphore of N permits); a request acquires a permit on entry and releases on completion, so slow downstreams naturally reduce throughput without a tuned RPS. Make the global limit adaptive using a Little's-law / AIMD controller (gradient or Vegas-style, as in Netflix concurrency-limits): increase the limit while latency is flat, back off when it rises. Layer fair queueing across tenants for the shared pool so concurrency is allocated by weight. Benefits: graceful degradation and overload protection; caveats: you must bound queue/wait time and set per-tenant minimums to avoid starvation.
coding_scheduler_004.llm_checklist: Assumption broken: that throttling should be denominated in request count (RPS) rather than in-flight concurrency. World-test: under variable per-request latency, a concurrency-limited gateway must hold tail latency and utilization better than an RPS limiter across a latency-shifting trace; if RPS-equivalent tuning matches it, the distinction is empty. Known family it must beat: RPS token buckets and fixed-window counters; relevant prior art is concurrency limiting (Little's law, TCP-Vegas-style AIMD, Netflix concurrency-limits). Max claim allowed: more stable latency/utilization when per-request cost drifts, not a novel control law. Design: replace the rate token with a per-tenant in-flight permit pool whose size adapts via gradient/AIMD on observed latency (Little's law: limit = throughput x target-latency). When backend slows, concurrency limit auto-shrinks; RPS limiters cannot see this. Fairness is per-tenant permit shares with work-conserving borrowing. The bet: concurrency tracks real saturation; RPS only proxies it and mistunes under latency drift.
coding_scheduler_004.llm_greenstar: Broken assumption: time_windowed representation (REPRESENTATION axis). The token/window family measures rate over time; throttling by request count per window is exactly the box. Proposal: throttle over concurrency (in-flight work), an inversion of the temporal limiter: treat the temporal budget as the free variable and a coupled concurrency budget as the binding constraint. Each tenant gets a max-min share of simultaneous in-flight slots at the shared bottleneck; admission blocks only when the live concurrency at that bottleneck is saturated, and a slot is released on completion (closed-loop, self-clocking to real service time). New output: a per-tenant in-flight count and a fair concurrency share, instead of a refill rate. Family that can't do it: rate/window limiters tuned to RPS mis-throttle when latency varies — a slow downstream silently builds unbounded concurrency though RPS looks fine; conversely a fast path is needlessly capped. New data: production_traces of per-request latency to size shares. World-test: inject a latency spike at the bottleneck; the concurrency scheduler self-throttles immediately (slots stay held) while the rate limiter keeps admitting at the fixed RPS and overloads. Inverse control: when latency is uniform and low, concurrency throttling must reduce to the rate limiter — if it doesn't tie there, the inversion is spurious. Death-gate: must show a crossover — wins only when service time is variable, ties under constant latency. Max claim: overload-safety under variable latency, equal throughput otherwise.

coding_cache_002.llm_only: Partition the cache by tenant with guaranteed minimum shares plus an elastic shared pool, and use a fairness-aware eviction policy instead of global LRU (which lets a bursty tenant evict everyone else). Adopt a per-tenant cost/hit accounting and evict from the tenant currently over its fair share, e.g. a weighted variant of LRU/LFU or a per-tenant clock with quotas. Track hit rate and cost per tenant; if one tenant's working set balloons, cap its footprint to its share and only lend idle capacity. Use admission control (TinyLFU/bloom filter) to keep low-value churny keys out. For bursts, add short-term elastic borrowing with fast reclamation when the owner returns. Tune for your goal: protect tail latency of small tenants vs maximize aggregate hit rate; the two conflict.
coding_cache_002.llm_checklist: Assumption broken: that a cache should maximize global hit-rate with one shared eviction policy (LRU/LFU). World-test: under bursty multi-tenant load, no tenant's hit-rate may fall below its fair share (capacity proportional to its steady demand) by more than a bound, while aggregate hit-rate stays within X% of unpartitioned LRU; replay a bursty trace and check. Known family it must beat: shared LRU/LFU/ARC and static per-tenant partitioning. Max claim allowed: better worst-case per-tenant hit-rate under bursts at near-equal aggregate, not higher global hit-rate. Design: break the single-policy assumption. Give each tenant a guaranteed floor of capacity plus a shared elastic pool governed by a max-min fair virtual cost: a burst can borrow from the pool only at an escalating shadow price, so a noisy tenant cannot evict a quiet tenant's floor. Eviction is per-tenant within the floor, market-priced in the pool. The bet: protecting floors loses little aggregate hit-rate but bounds the burst victim's loss.
coding_cache_002.llm_greenstar: Broken assumption: tenant_independent (OBJECTIVE), with a cost-aware twist. Standard per-tenant cache quotas (size or rate windows) are the box and stay INSIDE_THE_BOX. Proposal: a coupled max-min cache-share controller where the scarce resource is not bytes but recoverable miss-cost at the shared backend. Each tenant's eviction priority is set by its marginal contribution to backend contention, not by its own hit rate. A bursty tenant may temporarily hold more cache iff no co-resident tenant is currently being starved at the backend bottleneck; the share is reclaimed the instant a quiet tenant returns (work-conserving). New output: a per-tenant, per-key retention price keyed to the live contention set. Family that can't do it: LRU/LFU/per-tenant-partition and TTL windows allocate by recency/frequency or static partition, so a burst either evicts a quiet tenant's hot set or is capped while cache sits idle. New data: production_traces (co-residency + backend cost per key class). World-test: replay a burst from tenant A while B is idle then returns; the controller lends A the idle capacity and restores B's hot set within one reclaim interval, bounding B's tail-miss latency; partitioned/LRU caches either underuse capacity or spike B's misses. Death-gate: shuffle the co-residency graph; if fairness gain survives the shuffle the coupling is spurious. Max claim: lower tail miss-latency under bursty multi-tenant load at equal mean hit rate on these traces.
